images.caption: Figure 2:Behavioral separation in TLMs.Left:Validation-loss trajectories during private fine-tuning of the650650M TLM. The keyed configuration𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}improves on Spanish (private) data while remaining stable on English (public) data. The public configuration𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}shows no improvement on Spanish, confirming that private knowledge does not leak.Right:Instruction-following win rate for the650650M TLM on AlpacaEval, judged bygpt-oss-120b. The keyed configuration rapidly acquires instruction-following ability across all difficulty levels while the public configuration does not.

Figure 3:Left:Memorization of synthetic facts.Exact-match accuracy during private fine-tuning of TLM-180M.𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}reaches perfect recall of all400400facts;𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}remains at zero throughout.Right:Keyed-update frequency.Public-domain validation loss for TLM-180M pretrained with varying keyed-update frequencyff.𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}is unaffected byff;𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}improves asffdecreases, with diminishing returns beyondf=20f{=}20(∼5%{\sim}5\%additional FLOPs).

Table 1:Storage cost of a 1% LoRA adapter compared with a 5% permutation key across model scales. We use a 1% adapter since that is the size where it roughly matches our TLM’s performance as shown inFigure˜15in the Appendix. Parenthesized values report the LoRA-over-key size ratio.
Figure 4:Comparing public-domain validation loss during pretraining for TLM-180M against a non-tiered baseline.

Figure 5:Robustness to extraction attacks.Left:fine-tuning-based extraction.An attacker fine-tunes on50%50\%of the synthetic biographies through𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}(no key) and is evaluated on the held-out50%50\%. Three starting checkpoints are compared: a non-TLM baseline, a TLM before private fine-tuning, and a TLM whose𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}has memorized all400400biographies. All three memorize the training split at comparable rates (solid), with zero leakage on the held-out split (dashed) even after100100epochs.Right:Partial-key access.Random subsets of the full key are applied to TLM-180M; each point averages100100draws. Accuracy remains near zero until more than90%90\%of the key is known.

Figure 6:We start from the 180M cumulative multi-tier pretrained model with three 5% keys. The private datasets areD1=deu,D2=tur,D3=spa.D_{1}=\mathrm{deu},D_{2}=\mathrm{tur},D_{3}=\mathrm{spa}.Fine-tuning proceeds sequentially: stage 1 trainsC1C_{1}onD1D_{1}, stage 2 trainsC2C_{2}onD2D_{2}, and stage 3 trainsC3C_{3}onD3D_{3}. We report validation losses for each keyed configuration on the private domain of the corresponding stage.

Figure 7:We start from the 180M cumulative multi-tier pretrained model with three 5% keys. The private datasets areD1=deu,D2=tur,D3=spa.D_{1}=\mathrm{deu},D_{2}=\mathrm{tur},D_{3}=\mathrm{spa}.Each stage is trained on22B private tokens.

Figure 8:Pretraining trajectories for the 180M TLM for all key-sizes.Left: public-configuration validation loss under𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}.Right: keyed-configuration validation loss under𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}. Across key sizes, both configurations continue improving during pretraining, with larger keys showing slightly higher validation loss.

Figure 9:Pretraining trajectories for the 650M TLM at two key-sizes.Left: public-configuration validation loss under𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}.Right: keyed-configuration validation loss under𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}. Both key sizes continue improving throughout pretraining, with the 5% key reaching slightly lower validation loss than the 10% key on both configurations, consistent with the 180M trend.

Figure 10:Cumulative multi-tier pretraining.A shared model is trained with one public configuration𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}and three nested keyed configurations:𝒞K1\mathcal{C}_{K_{1}}applies key 1,𝒞K2\mathcal{C}_{K_{2}}applies keys1+21{+}2, and𝒞K3\mathcal{C}_{K_{3}}applies keys1+2+31{+}2{+}3. At each step, training uses𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}and one round-robin keyed configuration.

Figure 12:Left:Permuting weights of a pretrained model destroys capabilities.We apply random parameter permutations to Qwen-3-8B, allocating 25% of the swap budget to attention heads and 75% to MLP columns, and evaluate MMLU accuracy.Right:Tiered pretraining is necessary for the keyed configuration to function.Public-domain validation loss during private fine-tuning on22B Spanish tokens, comparing a TLM and a non-tiered baseline under𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}(dashed) and𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}(solid). In the non-tiered baseline,𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}starts with very high loss and recovers only partially, whereas both TLM configurations remain stable.

Figure 13:Left:Effect of keyed-update frequency on fine-tuning.Each180180M TLM fromFigure˜3(right) is fine-tuned on22B tokens of Spanish FineWeb2.𝒞K\mathcal{C}_{K}’s private-domain loss stays nearly flat across values offf, while𝒞pub\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{pub}}remains high, indicating preserved behavioral separation.Right:Private-domain performance: TLM vs. Non-TLM Baseline.Private-domain validation loss during fine-tuning on22B tokens of Spanish FineWeb2 data, comparing the keyed configuration of TLM-180M against a non-tiered baseline of the same architecture fine-tuned on the same data.
(a)Spanish FineWeb2 fine-tuning.
(a)Spanish FineWeb2 fine-tuning.
(b)Synbios and multilingual multi-stage fine-tuning.
Figure 15:LoRA and TLM training comparison.A 1% bf16 LoRA adapter closely matches the private-domain validation loss of the 5% keyed TLM during Spanish fine-tuning, making it a comparable baseline.

Figure 16:Left:Portuguese fine-tuning.Validation-loss trajectories for the 650M TLM fine-tuned on Portuguese private data.Right:Instruction fine-tuning.Validation-loss trajectories for the 650M TLM fine-tuned on Alpaca.

Figure 17:Behavioral separation under interleaved fine-tuning.Both panels follow the setup ofFigure˜2, with the KL anchor replaced by the interleaved-CE objective.Left: Spanish fine-tuning on the smaller180180M TLM.Right: Instruction fine-tuning under the same setting asFigure˜2.
